content: The best problem-solvers are actually great problem-finders. The premium skill in innovation is not solution generation — it is knowing which problem is worth solving. Organizations that skip the problem-framing phase are optimizing the wrong function, investing enormous energy solving brilliantly-defined wrong problems.
implication: Before your next sprint or brainstorm, invest deliberate time in the question space. Ask whether you have correctly identified the problem before mobilizing resources to solve it. Reframing the question is often worth more than any solution you could generate.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters — Perry Klebahn & Jeremy Utley

content: Most innovation failures are fundamentally framing failures. Teams invest enormous energy solving the wrong problem brilliantly. The discipline of problem framing — sitting with the question, reframing from multiple angles, designing 'How might we?' prompts before generating solutions — is the highest-leverage skill in the entire innovation toolkit.
implication: The moment a team jumps from 'here's the problem' to 'let's brainstorm solutions' is often the moment innovation fails. Build a deliberate pause — a problem-framing phase — before any solution-generation activity. The quality of the question determines the ceiling of every answer.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters — Perry Klebahn & Jeremy Utley

content: The 'How Might We?' prompt is not just a linguistic convention — it is a structural move that holds three critical design choices simultaneously. 'How' assumes there is a solution. 'Might' removes the pressure of correctness and invites possibility. 'We' signals that this is a collaborative challenge. Together, the phrase opens a generative space that neither a closed question nor a statement of the problem can create.
implication: Swap declarative problem statements for 'How Might We?' prompts before any creative session. The linguistic structure of the question is not cosmetic — it is psychologically load-bearing. The right prompt design can double the creative output of the same group of people.
source_ref: AI and Design Thinking with Stanford's Jeremy Utley — Bloom Mood Podcast

content: Spending time in the question space is not indecision — it is the highest-leverage phase of the creative process. Great innovators are disproportionately patient with questions and impatient with premature answers. The rush to solutions is a form of creative impatience that systematically produces inferior outcomes.
implication: Reframe 'dwelling in questions' as a productive activity, not a delay. Schedule and protect time for question exploration explicitly — make it a visible, calendared phase of the innovation process rather than something that gets compressed under deadline pressure.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters — Perry Klebahn & Jeremy Utley

content: Problem finding is a trainable skill, not an innate gift. Just as Utley argues that creativity itself is a learnable discipline, the ability to identify and frame the right problem can be developed through deliberate practice — specifically through the habit of generating multiple reframings of the same challenge before committing to any single definition.
implication: Before accepting the first formulation of a problem, generate at least five alternative framings. Each reframing reveals different constraints, different stakeholders, and different solution spaces. The team that practices reframing as a discipline will consistently out-innovate the team that takes problem definitions at face value.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck Podcast

content: The way a problem is framed determines the solution space before a single idea is generated. A narrowly framed question produces incremental answers; a broadly framed question opens exponentially more solution territory. Question design is therefore a form of upstream architecture — it determines the shape of everything downstream.
implication: Treat the framing of a challenge as a design artifact in its own right — something to be iterated, tested, and refined. Share multiple framings of the same problem with your team and observe how the solution ideas shift. The best frame is the one that unleashes the most creative energy.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters — Perry Klebahn & Jeremy Utley

content: Most organizations treat the problem definition as a given — handed down from leadership, inherited from last quarter's strategy, or assumed from customer complaints. This is the structural source of most innovation misfires. The question itself needs to be questioned. Utley's design thinking framework begins not with divergent solution generation but with divergent problem generation — surfacing multiple competing definitions of what the real challenge is.
implication: Make problem generation — not just solution generation — an explicit team activity. Bring your team together to generate ten different framings of your current strategic challenge before you brainstorm any solutions. The reframe you uncover is often worth more than any answer you could have found.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: AI dramatically amplifies the leverage of good question design while exposing the cost of bad question design. A precisely framed prompt to an AI system yields dramatically superior outputs compared to a vague one. The human skill that AI makes most visible — and most valuable — is the ability to ask the right question. Problem finding becomes the primary differentiator in an AI-augmented world.
original_quote: The scarce resource shifts entirely to the front end: insight, framing, taste, and the ability to ask questions that machines can't generate on their own.
implication: As AI handles execution, the return on investment for question design skills increases dramatically. Leaders and builders who develop the discipline of precise, creative problem framing will extract exponentially more value from AI tools than those who use AI as a vague brainstorming partner.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Reframing is not just a creative technique — it is a diagnostic tool. When a team is stuck, the most productive intervention is rarely more brainstorming; it is stepping back to ask whether the problem has been correctly identified. Utley consistently applies this move in his Stanford d.school teaching: the breakthrough often comes not from a better answer but from a better question.
implication: When your team hits a creative wall, resist the instinct to push harder on solutions. Instead, run a reframing exercise: 'What if the problem isn't X but actually Y?' The most valuable 30 minutes in a stuck project is often spent questioning the question itself.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders Podcast

content: The distinction between problem-finding and problem-solving maps directly onto the distinction between leading and lagging indicators in Utley's broader framework. Problem-finding is the upstream, generative activity that determines the quality of everything downstream. Investing in problem-finding capacity is the innovation equivalent of investing in a leading indicator.
implication: Measure how much time your team invests in problem-finding versus problem-solving. If your ratio heavily favors solving over finding, you are likely working on well-optimized answers to the wrong questions. Rebalance deliberately.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters — Perry Klebahn & Jeremy Utley

content: One of Utley's core teaching moves at the Stanford d.school is separating the generative and evaluative phases of thinking — and this applies equally to the problem space. Teams tend to evaluate problem definitions before they have generated enough of them. The result is premature lock-in on the first plausible problem framing rather than the best one.
implication: Apply the same 'no judgment' rule to problem generation that you apply to idea generation. Generate a large number of possible problem definitions before evaluating any of them. Premature commitment to a problem frame is as costly as premature commitment to a solution.
source_ref: AI and Design Thinking with Stanford's Jeremy Utley — Bloom Mood Podcast

content: The 'How Might We?' question format is specifically designed to sit at the correct level of abstraction — not so broad that it gives no direction, not so narrow that it forecloses the solution space. Calibrating question breadth is itself a skill: too broad ('How might we improve the world?') and you get paralysis; too narrow ('How might we add a blue button?') and you get iteration, not innovation.
implication: Practice tuning your 'How Might We?' prompts to the right level of scope. A well-calibrated question is broad enough to allow surprising solutions but specific enough to point creative energy in a productive direction. Test your question by asking: does it invite multiple meaningfully different answers? If not, widen it.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley on Why Mastering Ideaflow Elevates Everything Else — Passion Struck Podcast

content: Utley draws a sharp distinction between the problem a customer articulates and the problem they actually have. The articulated complaint is a symptom; the real problem is often one level deeper. Design thinking's empathy and observation phases exist precisely to surface the underlying challenge that customers cannot self-diagnose — the gap between the reported problem and the real one is where genuine innovation lives.
implication: Never accept the first problem statement a customer, user, or stakeholder offers. Use empathy interviews, observation, and 'five whys' techniques to move from the symptom to the root cause. The most valuable insight is almost never the one that comes first.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show

content: Asking better questions is not just a facilitation technique — it is a competitive advantage. In a world where execution is increasingly automated, the ability to identify the right problem to work on becomes the primary source of strategic differentiation. Utley argues that AI will make this even more pronounced: the humans who win will be those who can ask questions that machines cannot generate.
implication: Invest in question-asking as a formal organizational capability — not just a soft skill. Train facilitators, build question-generation rituals into innovation processes, and evaluate leaders partly on the quality of the questions they ask, not just the decisions they make.
source_ref: The Rising Opportunity Cost of Being Human — Methods of the Masters blog

content: The board — or any leadership body that has been insulated from frontline experience — often cannot imagine futures it hasn't lived. This is a problem-finding deficit: if the people responsible for identifying strategic challenges are disconnected from emerging realities, the organization will systematically misframe its most important questions. Problem-finding requires proximity to the problem.
implication: Leaders who want to find better problems must stay close to the ground: customers, emerging technologies, adjacent industries, and their own team's daily friction. Strategic distance from the problem produces strategic misframing. Build deliberate feedback loops that keep leadership in contact with ground-level reality.
source_ref: Your Board Can't Imagine the Future — Methods of the Masters blog

content: Utley consistently points out that the question 'How might we?' is not just a brainstorming opener — it is a commitment to a specific cognitive mode. Entering a meeting with a 'How might we?' question signals to every participant that the room is in generative, not evaluative, mode. This environmental signal changes the quality of participation before a single idea is offered.
implication: Use the explicit language of 'How Might We?' as an environmental design tool, not just a semantic one. When you open a meeting with a HMW question instead of a problem statement, you change the psychological contract of the room. People arrive to generate, not to defend.
source_ref: From Bonehead to Brilliant: Innovation through Ideaflow — TD.org Accidental Trainer Podcast

content: Utley frames AI prompting as a direct extension of question design discipline. The people who get extraordinary outputs from AI are not the ones with the best technical knowledge of the tool — they are the ones who have developed the human skill of precise, creative question formulation. Prompt engineering is problem framing applied to a new medium.
implication: Frame your AI practice as question design practice. Every interaction with an AI tool is a repetition in the discipline of problem formulation. Teams that use AI heavily will, over time, either get better or worse at asking questions — the outcome depends on whether they treat prompting as a thoughtless habit or a deliberate craft.
source_ref: Be the Prompt — Jeremy's Newsletter (Substack)

content: There is a systematic organizational bias toward solution space over problem space. Meetings are structured to evaluate options, not to interrogate the question. Budgets are allocated to execution, not to exploration. Timelines reward delivery, not discovery. This structural bias means organizations are systematically under-investing in the phase of innovation where the highest leverage lives.
implication: Audit your meeting structures, budget allocations, and timeline incentives for bias toward solution space. If you have no formally protected time for problem-finding and question exploration, you have structurally guaranteed that your team will work on the wrong problems efficiently.
source_ref: Ideaflow: The Only Business Metric That Matters — Perry Klebahn & Jeremy Utley

content: Utley's design thinking pedagogy at the Stanford d.school treats the problem brief not as a starting constraint but as a provisional hypothesis. The initial problem statement is a place to begin exploration, not a specification to execute against. This distinction — between a fixed brief and a generative hypothesis — is foundational to creative problem-finding.
implication: Treat your initial problem brief as a hypothesis, not a contract. Build in explicit checkpoints to revisit and revise the problem definition as you learn more. The most successful innovation projects are often the ones that ended up solving a different problem than they started with.
source_ref: AI and Design Thinking with Stanford's Jeremy Utley — Bloom Mood Podcast

content: One of Utley's recurring observations is that the questions leaders ask in meetings determine the quality of thinking in their organizations. A leader who asks 'What's the solution?' gets answers; a leader who asks 'What's the real problem here?' gets insight. Question design is a leadership behavior, not just a facilitation technique — and it cascades through the entire organization.
implication: Audit the questions you ask in your own meetings for the next two weeks. Are you asking for solutions or interrogating the problem? The questions you ask most frequently become the cognitive defaults of your team. Leaders who are great question-askers build organizations that are great at finding the right problems.
source_ref: Ideaflow: Achieving Breakthrough Creativity and Ideas — We Are Human Leaders Podcast

content: Utley argues that the imagination ceiling — the limit on what people can envision as possible — is often located in the problem statement itself. When a problem is framed in terms of existing categories ('How do we improve our current product?'), it activates only existing knowledge. When it is framed in terms of desired outcomes ('How might we make our customers feel X?'), it opens access to a much wider range of creative possibilities.
implication: Shift your problem statements from process-oriented to outcome-oriented. Instead of 'How do we fix our onboarding flow?' ask 'How might we make new users feel capable and excited within their first five minutes?' Outcome framing breaks the gravitational pull of existing solutions.
source_ref: The Imagination Ceiling: Why 70% of Your People Are Stuck on AI — Methods of the Masters blog

content: The reason most companies have a generation problem rather than an evaluation problem applies equally to the question space. Organizations have robust processes for evaluating which problems to prioritize — roadmaps, OKRs, strategy reviews — but almost no process for generating a rich set of possible problem framings in the first place. The funnel is wide at evaluation and narrow at problem generation, when it should be the reverse.
implication: Flip your innovation funnel. Generate many possible framings of your strategic challenge before applying any prioritization or evaluation criteria. The OKR you set is only as good as the problem-finding process that preceded it. Most strategic misfires are upstream of the roadmap.
source_ref: Methods of the Masters blog — Jeremy Utley

content: Utley connects the skill of problem-finding directly to the capacity for empathy — the ability to see a situation from a perspective other than your own. The reason experts often miss the real problem is not lack of intelligence but lack of beginner's eyes. The most valuable problem-finders are those who can temporarily suspend their expertise and see a challenge the way a newcomer — or a user — would see it.
implication: Build structured empathy practices into your problem-finding process: user interviews, job shadows, customer immersions. The goal is not just to gather data but to reset your own perception of what the problem actually is. Expertise is an asset in solution space and a liability in problem space.
source_ref: Jeremy Utley: Co-Author of Ideaflow & Director of Stanford's d.school — Kara Goldin Show
